mission.title: cilium: policy: return ICMP "Destination unreachable" on ipv4 egress policy denials
mission.description:
# Description
This PR adds a new flag `--policy-deny-response` to control the way we reject packets when they are getting a policy denial. There are two options:
- `none` - this is the behavior we have today, packets are dropped silently. This can cause clients to timeout indefinitely (see more context in https://github.com/cilium/cilium/issues/17944)
- `icmp` - **new feature**: this sends an ICMP "Destination Unreachable / Packet Filtered" response to the client so that they can kill the connection much quicker

The implementation in this PR is for ipv4 egress traffic only. ipv6 and ingress will be added later on in separate PRs.

This relies very heavily on code from https://github.com/cilium/cilium/pull/28157 which implemented very similar kind of behavior for services with no backends. I simply re-used the same code for ICMP packet generation and put it in the policy denial logic.

I didn't implement the TCP RST behavior from the original issue, I think the ICMP response should be

resolution.summary:
> * Before I continue any further with tests, docs, Helm values, etc. I'd like to get some feedback on whether this approach generally makes sense.

The approach looks good to me :)

Should we return icmp-port-unreachable for L4 drops?

> * If the approach makes sense, I'm also curious to know what would be the best way to test this behavior. Should I add something in `bpf/tests` or e2e tests or both?

IMO, `bpf/tests` is enough to validate this.

> FWIW I can see that [there's rate-limiting](https://github.com/cilium/cilium/blob/86dd1d02444f4d5b6c826af234f7f37432563398/bpf/lib/lb.h#L1997) only for the ICMPv6 version of the `SERVICE_NO_BACKEND_RESPONSE` feature but not for ICMPv4

That doesn't look expected to me. We might need to discuss/track it into a dedicated issue. cc @dylandreimerink
steps: Review the issue discussion for context
Apply the fix from the linked pull request
codeSnippets:
# curl --connect-timeout 5 google.com
curl: (28) Failed to connect to google.com port 80: Connection timed out

# curl google.com
curl: (7) Failed to connect to google.com port 80: No route to host

# ping 142.250.75.238
PING 142.250.75.238 (142.250.75.238) 56(84) bytes of data.
From 142.250.75.238 icmp_seq=1 Packet filtered
From 142.250.75.238 icmp_seq=2 Packet filtered
[...]
